title_en: The Mu'tazili Framework: Theological Positions and Their Influence
content_en:
To understand Al-Kashaf responsibly, one must understand the Mu'tazili theological framework that shapes it. The Mu'tazilah held a distinctive cluster of theological positions that set them apart from the Sunni mainstream — positions on divine attributes, divine justice, human free will, and the status of the Quran that al-Zamakhshari wove into his exegesis at crucial points.

The most consequential of these positions for Quranic interpretation is the Mu'tazili doctrine that the Quran is created (makhluq). This doctrine, which provoked the famous Mihna (Inquisition) under the Abbasid caliph al-Ma'mun in the third Islamic century — during which Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal was imprisoned for refusing to affirm it — stands in direct opposition to the Sunni consensus that the Quran is the uncreated speech of God. Al-Zamakhshari's commentary on verses touching on the divine word and the nature of revelation reflects this Mu'tazili position, sometimes explicitly and sometimes through his choice of interpretive alternatives.

The second major Mu'tazili influence is the doctrine of divine justice (al-adl), which the Mu'tazilah interpreted in a specific rationalist direction: God cannot do what is contrary to justice as reason perceives it. This led them to deny predestination in the Sunni sense and to emphasize human free will and moral accountability in a way that shapes how al-Zamakhshari reads verses about divine guidance, misguidance, and the sealing of hearts. Where Sunni exegetes read divine agency, al-Zamakhshari often reads human choice — interpreting God's 'sealing' of hearts as a description of what humans have done to themselves rather than a direct divine act.

The third influence is the Mu'tazili position on divine attributes (al-sifat): they denied the independent reality of the divine attributes (knowledge, power, will, etc.) and interpreted them as expressions of the divine essence, to avoid what they saw as a form of multiplicity in the divine being. This shapes how al-Zamakhshari handles the asma al-husna (divine names) and the attribute verses in the Quran.

Sunni scholars — Ash'ari, Maturidi, and Athari alike — identified and catalogued these theological interventions. Ibn al-Munir al-Iskandari (d. 683 AH), a Maliki scholar, wrote Al-Intisaf min Al-Kashaf (The Vindication Against Al-Kashaf), a comprehensive response to al-Zamakhshari's Mu'tazili glosses, refuting them from Sunni positions while quoting the Kashaf text verbatim. This work is often printed alongside Al-Kashaf in traditional editions, giving the reader both al-Zamakhshari's positions and the Sunni scholarly response on the same page.

Later critical readers include al-Taftazani, who addressed Al-Kashaf's theological positions in his commentary on theology, and numerous scholars in the Ottoman and Mughal scholarly traditions who incorporated Al-Kashaf into their tafsir curricula while requiring students to study its theological passages alongside the Sunni critiques. This parallel reading model — study the text, study the critique — became the standard method for engaging with Al-Kashaf in traditional Islamic education, and remains the appropriate model today.
